From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Code for arrangement of locomotive wheels

For the term "white notation" in music, see [Mensural notation](/wiki/Mensural_notation "Mensural notation").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article **may contain [original research](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")**. Please [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Whyte_notation&action=edit) by [verifying](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability") the claims made and adding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources"). Statements consisting only of original research should be removed. *(September 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Locotypes.gif/250px-Locotypes.gif)](/wiki/File:Locotypes.gif)

A selection of early 20th century locomotive types according to their Whyte notation and their comparative size

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Locomotive_classification--Colvin_1906--300dpi.jpg/250px-Locomotive_classification--Colvin_1906--300dpi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Locomotive_classification--Colvin_1906--300dpi.jpg)

Whyte notation from a handbook for railroad industry workers published in 1906[[1]](#cite_note-railroad_pocket-book-1)

The **Whyte notation** is a classification method for [steam locomotives](/wiki/Steam_locomotives "Steam locomotives"), and some internal combustion locomotives and [electric locomotives](/wiki/Electric_locomotives "Electric locomotives"), by [wheel arrangement](/wiki/Wheel_arrangement "Wheel arrangement"). It was devised by [Frederick Methvan Whyte](/wiki/Frederick_Methvan_Whyte "Frederick Methvan Whyte"),[[2]](#cite_note-steam_glossary-2) and came into use in the early twentieth century following a December 1900 editorial in *American Engineer and Railroad Journal*.

The notation was adopted and remains in use in North America and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") to describe the wheel arrangements of [steam locomotives](/wiki/Steam_locomotive "Steam locomotive"), but for modern [locomotives](/wiki/Locomotive "Locomotive"), [multiple units](/wiki/Multiple_unit "Multiple unit") and [trams](/wiki/Tram "Tram") it has been supplanted by the [UIC system](/wiki/UIC_classification_of_locomotive_axle_arrangements "UIC classification of locomotive axle arrangements") in Europe and by the [AAR system](/wiki/AAR_wheel_arrangement "AAR wheel arrangement") (essentially a simplification of the UIC system) in North America. However, geared steam locomotives do not use the notation. They are classified by their model and their number of trucks.

## Structure of the system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Whyte_notation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Structure of the system")]

### Basic form

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Whyte_notation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Basic form")]

The notation in its basic form counts the number of [leading wheels](/wiki/Leading_wheels "Leading wheels"), then the number of [driving wheels](/wiki/Driving_wheels "Driving wheels"), and finally the number of [trailing wheels](/wiki/Trailing_wheels "Trailing wheels"), numbers being separated by dashes.[[3]](#cite_note-3) For example, a locomotive with two leading axles (four wheels) in front, then three driving axles (six wheels) and then one trailing axle (two wheels) is classified as a [4-6-2](/wiki/4-6-2 "4-6-2") locomotive, and is commonly known as a *Pacific*.

### Denotion of other locomotives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Whyte_notation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Denotion of other locomotives")]

#### Articulated locomotives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Whyte_notation&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Articulated locomotives")]

For [articulated locomotives](/wiki/Articulated_locomotives "Articulated locomotives") that have two wheelsets, such as [Garratts](/wiki/Garratt_locomotive "Garratt locomotive"), which are effectively two locomotives joined by a common [boiler](/wiki/Boiler "Boiler"), each wheelset is denoted separately, with a plus sign (+) between them. Thus a 4-6-2-type Garratt is a [4-6-2+2-6-4](/wiki/4-6-2%2B2-6-4 "4-6-2+2-6-4"). For Garratt locomotives, the plus sign is used even when there are no intermediate unpowered wheels, e.g. the [LMS Garratt](/wiki/LMS_Garratt "LMS Garratt") [2-6-0+0-6-2](/wiki/2-6-0%2B0-6-2 "2-6-0+0-6-2"). This is because the two engine units are more than just [power bogies](/wiki/Power_bogie "Power bogie"). They are complete engines, carrying fuel and water tanks. The plus sign represents the bridge (carrying the boiler) that links the two engines.

Simpler articulated types, such as [Mallets](/wiki/Mallet_locomotive "Mallet locomotive"), have a jointed frame under a common boiler where there are no unpowered wheels between the sets of powered wheels. Typically, the forward frame is free to swing, whereas the rear frame is rigid with the boiler. Thus, a [Union Pacific Big Boy](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Big_Boy "Union Pacific Big Boy") is a [4-8-8-4](/wiki/4-8-8-4 "4-8-8-4"): four leading wheels, one group of eight driving wheels, another group of eight driving wheels, and then four trailing wheels. Sometimes articulated locomotives of this type are denoted with a “+” between each driving wheels set (so in the previous case, the Big Boy would be a 4-8+8-4). This may have been developed to distinguish articulated and duplex arrangements; duplex arrangements would get a “-“ being rigid and articulated locomotives would get a “+” being flexible. However, given all the wheel arrangements for duplex locomotives have been mutually exclusive to them, it is usually considered unnecessary and thus another “-“ is usually used.

[Triplex locomotives](/wiki/Triplex_locomotive "Triplex locomotive"), and any theoretical larger ones, simply expand on basic articulated locomotives, for example, [2-8-8-8-2](/wiki/2-8-8-8-2 "2-8-8-8-2"). In the case of the Belgian [quadruplex locomotive](/wiki/Quadruplex_locomotive "Quadruplex locomotive"), the arrangement is listed as 0-6-2+2-4-2-4-2+2-6-0.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

#### Duplex locomotives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Whyte_notation&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Duplex locomotives")]

For [duplex locomotives](/wiki/Duplex_locomotive "Duplex locomotive"), which have two sets of coupled driving wheels mounted rigidly on the same frame, the same method is used as for [Mallet](/wiki/Mallet_(locomotive) "Mallet (locomotive)") [articulated locomotives](#Articulated_locomotives) – the number of leading wheels is placed first, followed by the leading set of driving wheels, followed by the trailing set of driving wheels, followed by the trailing wheels, each number being separated by a hyphen.

#### Tank locomotives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Whyte_notation&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Tank locomotives")]

A number of standard suffixes can be used to extend the Whyte notation for [tank locomotives](/wiki/Tank_locomotives "Tank locomotives"):[[5]](#cite_note-irs-5)

| Suffix | Meaning | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [No Suffix] | [Tender locomotive](/wiki/Tender_locomotive "Tender locomotive") | [0-6-0](/wiki/0-6-0 "0-6-0") |
| T | [Tank locomotive](/wiki/Tank_locomotive "Tank locomotive") | [0-6-2](/wiki/0-6-2 "0-6-2")[T](/wiki/Side_tank_locomotive "Side tank locomotive") |
| ST | [Saddle tank locomotive](/wiki/Saddle_tank_locomotive "Saddle tank locomotive") | [0-4-0](/wiki/0-4-0 "0-4-0")[ST](/wiki/Saddle_tank_locomotive "Saddle tank locomotive") |
| WT | [Well tank locomotive](/wiki/Well_tank_locomotive "Well tank locomotive") | [0-4-0](/wiki/0-4-0 "0-4-0")[WT](/wiki/Well_tank_locomotive "Well tank locomotive") |
| PT | [Pannier tank locomotive](/wiki/Pannier_tank_locomotive "Pannier tank locomotive") | [0-6-0](/wiki/0-6-0 "0-6-0")[PT](/wiki/Pannier_tank_locomotive "Pannier tank locomotive") |
| C or CT | [Crane tank locomotive](/wiki/Crane_tank_locomotive "Crane tank locomotive") | [0-6-2](/wiki/0-6-2 "0-6-2")[CT](/wiki/Crane_tank_locomotive "Crane tank locomotive") |
| IST | [Inverted saddle tank locomotive](/wiki/Inverted_saddle_tank_locomotive "Inverted saddle tank locomotive") | [0-4-2](/wiki/0-4-2 "0-4-2")[IST](/wiki/Inverted_saddle_tank_locomotive "Inverted saddle tank locomotive") |
| T+T (or ST+T, WT+T, etc.) | [Tender-tank locomotive](/wiki/Tank_locomotive#Tender-tank "Tank locomotive") | [0-4-0](/wiki/0-4-0 "0-4-0")[T](/wiki/Side_tank_locomotive "Side tank locomotive")+[T](/wiki/Tender_(rail) "Tender (rail)") |
| WT | [Wing tank locomotive](/wiki/Tank_locomotive#Wing_tank "Tank locomotive") | [0-4-0](/wiki/0-4-0 "0-4-0")[WT](/wiki/Tank_locomotive#Wing_tank "Tank locomotive") |
| RT | [Rear tank locomotive](/wiki/Tank_locomotive#Rear_tank "Tank locomotive") | [0-4-4](/wiki/0-4-4T "0-4-4T")[RT](/wiki/Tank_locomotive#Rear_tank "Tank locomotive") |

#### Other steam locomotives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Whyte_notation&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Other steam locomotives")]

Various other types of steam locomotive can be also denoted through suffixes:[[5]](#cite_note-irs-5)

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| VB or VBT | | [Vertical boilered locomotive](/wiki/Vertical_boilered_locomotive "Vertical boilered locomotive") | [0-6-0](/wiki/0-6-0 "0-6-0")[VB](/wiki/Vertical_boilered_locomotive "Vertical boilered locomotive") |
| F | | [Fireless locomotive](/wiki/Fireless_locomotive "Fireless locomotive") | [0-6-0](/wiki/0-6-0 "0-6-0")[F](/wiki/Fireless_locomotive "Fireless locomotive") |
| CA | | [Compressed air locomotive](/wiki/Compressed_air_locomotive "Compressed air locomotive") | [0-6-0](/wiki/0-6-0 "0-6-0")[CA](/wiki/Compressed-air_locomotive "Compressed-air locomotive") |
| R | | [Railcar](/wiki/Railcar "Railcar") | [0-4-4-0](/wiki/0-4-4-0 "0-4-4-0")[R](/wiki/Railcar "Railcar") |
| R or RT | | [Rack locomotive](/wiki/Rack_locomotive "Rack locomotive") | [0-4-0](/wiki/0-4-0 "0-4-0")[RT](/wiki/Rack_locomotive "Rack locomotive") |

#### Internal combustion locomotives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Whyte_notation&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Internal combustion locomotives")]

"8w (locomotive)" redirects here. For other uses, see [8W (disambiguation) § Rail transport](/wiki/8W_(disambiguation)#Rail_transport "8W (disambiguation)").

The wheel arrangement of small [diesel](/wiki/Diesel_locomotive "Diesel locomotive") and [petrol](/wiki/Petrol_locomotive "Petrol locomotive") locomotives can be classified using the same notation as steam locomotives, e.g. 0-4-0, 0-6-0, 0-8-0. Where the axles are coupled by chains or shafts (rather than side rods) or are individually driven, the terms 4w (4-*wheeled*), 6w (6-wheeled) or 8w (8-wheeled) are generally used. For larger locomotives, the [UIC classification](/wiki/UIC_classification "UIC classification") is more commonly used.

Various suffixes are also used to denote the different types of internal combustion locomotives:[[5]](#cite_note-irs-5)

| Suffix | Meaning | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| PM | [Petrol-mechanical locomotive](/wiki/Petrol-mechanical_locomotive "Petrol-mechanical locomotive") | [4w](#Internal_combustion_locomotives)[PM](/wiki/Petrol-mechanical_locomotive "Petrol-mechanical locomotive") |
| PE | [Petrol-electric locomotive](/wiki/Petrol-electric_locomotive "Petrol-electric locomotive") | [0-6-0](/wiki/0-6-0 "0-6-0")[PE](/wiki/Petrol-electric_locomotive "Petrol-electric locomotive") |
| D | [Diesel locomotive](/wiki/Diesel_locomotive "Diesel locomotive") | [6w](#Internal_combustion_locomotives)[D](/wiki/Diesel_locomotive "Diesel locomotive") |
| DM | [Diesel–mechanical locomotive](/wiki/Diesel%E2%80%93mechanical_locomotive "Diesel–mechanical locomotive") | [8w](#Internal_combustion_locomotives)[DM](/wiki/Diesel%E2%80%93mechanical_locomotive "Diesel–mechanical locomotive") |
| DE | [Diesel–electric locomotive](/wiki/Diesel%E2%80%93electric_locomotive "Diesel–electric locomotive") | [0-4-0](/wiki/0-4-0 "0-4-0")[DE](/wiki/Diesel%E2%80%93electric_locomotive "Diesel–electric locomotive") |
| DH | [Diesel–hydraulic locomotive](/wiki/Diesel%E2%80%93hydraulic_locomotive "Diesel–hydraulic locomotive") | [0-6-0](/wiki/0-6-0 "0-6-0")[DH](/wiki/Diesel%E2%80%93hydraulic_locomotive "Diesel–hydraulic locomotive") |

#### Electric locomotives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Whyte_notation&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Electric locomotives")]

The wheel arrangement of small [electric locomotives](/wiki/Electric_locomotive "Electric locomotive") can be denoted using this notation, like with [internal combustion locomotives](#Internal_combustion_locomotives).

Suffixes used for electric locomotives include:

| Suffix | Meaning | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| BE | [Battery-electric locomotive](/wiki/Battery-electric_locomotive "Battery-electric locomotive") | [4w](#Internal_combustion_locomotives)[BE](/wiki/Battery_electric_locomotive "Battery electric locomotive") |
| OE | [Overhead-lines electric locomotive](/wiki/Electric_locomotive#Overhead_lines "Electric locomotive") | [0-8-0](/wiki/0-8-0 "0-8-0")[OE](/wiki/Electric_locomotive#Overhead_lines "Electric locomotive") |
| RE | [Third rail electric locomotive](/wiki/Third_rail "Third rail") | [4w](#Internal_combustion_locomotives)RE |

## Wheel arrangement names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Whyte_notation&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Wheel arrangement names")]

In American (and to a lesser extent British) practice, most wheel arrangements in common use were given names, sometimes from the name of the first such locomotive built. For example, the [2-2-0](/wiki/2-2-0 "2-2-0") type arrangement is named *Planet*, after [the 1830 locomotive](/wiki/Planet_(locomotive) "Planet (locomotive)") on which it was first used. (This naming convention is similar to the naming of warship classes.) Note that several wheel arrangements had multiple names, and some names were only used in some countries.

Wheel arrangements under the Whyte system are listed below. In the diagrams, the front of the locomotive is to the left.

| Arrangement (locomotive front is to the left) | Whyte classification | Name | No. of units produced |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
